name: 4-Bit Binary Counter
description: A 4-bit asynchronous binary counter built using JK flip flops. Counts from 0 to 15 (0000 to 1111) in binary. Each flip flop toggles on the falling edge of the previous stage, creating a ripple effect. The clock input drives the counter, and outputs Q0-Q3 represent the binary count value.

steps.instruction: Welcome! A binary counter counts in binary from 0 to 15. It uses four JK flip flops connected in a chain.
steps.expected_action: Observe the four JK flip flops connected in sequence.

steps.instruction: Each JK flip flop toggles its output on each clock pulse. The Q output of one flip flop clocks the next, creating a ripple counter.
steps.expected_action: Notice the NOT gates inverting the clock for each stage.

steps.instruction: FF0 (Q0) is the LSB and toggles on every clock pulse. It changes state at the highest frequency.
steps.expected_action: Pulse the clock and watch Q0 toggle every time.

steps.instruction: FF1 (Q1) toggles when Q0 goes from 1 to 0. FF2 toggles when Q1 goes from 1 to 0. FF3 toggles when Q2 goes from 1 to 0.
steps.expected_action: Pulse the clock several times and observe the ripple effect.

steps.instruction: Read the binary count from Q3Q2Q1Q0. The sequence is: 0000, 0001, 0010, 0011, 0100... up to 1111, then it wraps to 0000.
steps.expected_action: Count the pulses and verify the binary output matches the count.

steps.instruction: The Reset input clears all flip flops to 0 simultaneously. Use it to restart counting from zero.
steps.expected_action: Set Reset=1 to clear the counter, then Reset=0 to resume.

steps.instruction: Well done! Binary counters are used everywhere: digital clocks, frequency dividers, program counters in CPUs, and timer circuits.
steps.expected_action: You now understand sequential counting circuits!
